topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The morning sun felt like a personal affront, glaring off the already stained pavement. I was attempting to read the newspaper, the paper rustling under my fingertips with unnecessary loudness, when the hammering started. It was relentless, a sharp, insistent rhythm that drilled straight through the thin walls of my living room. I lowered the paper slowly, my jaw tightening. I could hear Mr. Henderson across the street, his voice a grating, cheerful bellow competing with the metallic clang. "Just a little project, old chap! A bit of boundary definition!" I squeezed my eyes shut for a moment, wishing the noise would simply cease. My shoulders slumped, a heavy, defeated weight settling over my frame. This was precisely the kind of unsolicited activity that made the air thick and unpleasant.

[story 2]

I watched from behind the dusty windowpane as the lumber truck arrived. Every piece of machinery seemed to vibrate with an irritating energy. I rubbed the bridge of my nose with two fingers, a gesture of profound weariness. The sight of the freshly cut wood, stacked haphazardly against Mr. Henderson’s property line, only intensified the tightness in my chest. I made a low sound, somewhere between a sigh and a warning rumble, directed vaguely toward the street. "Can you perhaps keep the racket down?" I muttered, the words tasting like ash. I didn't wait for a reply; I just turned my back, focusing intently on the pattern of cracks in the ceiling, trying to mentally construct a fortress against the intrusion.

[story 3]

When the surveyor showed up, I stood by the mailbox, arms crossed so tightly my knuckles were white. The man was all bright smiles and measured tones, entirely at odds with the sullen atmosphere I was projecting. "Just finalizing the measurements, sir," he explained, his voice annoyingly smooth. I didn't offer him a greeting. My gaze was fixed on the distant horizon, refusing to acknowledge the encroachment on my peace. Every time a shovel scraped against the dry earth nearby, I flinched slightly, a subtle tightening around my mouth. I felt a profound urge to retreat entirely, to become invisible, to simply cease existing in this shared space.
